content.assessment: The Finnish trajectory shows that leaving rural school survival to ad hoc administrative discretion and informal political reversal — rather than codified, rule-based criteria — is precisely the pattern Finland moved away from during 1972-1977 comprehensivization, when rural viability was treated as a precondition for system legitimacy, not a residual outcome of local bargaining. Finland's later experience (1995-2003) also shows that even a system with strong initial comprehensive commitments can rapidly lose small rural schools once low pupil-minimum protections are politically withdrawn, confirming that protections are reversible policy choices, not automatic features. T1's reliance on uneven local social capital to determine outcomes replicates a governance vacuum Finland deliberately filled with policy, and the Hungarian church-sector segregation risk under status quo (F-live-finnish_reform-09) sits alongside Finland's own contested segregation trajectory under choice/decentralization, reinforcing that unmanaged drift tends toward segregation regardless of country.
opportunities: Could still adopt Finland's model of treating rural viability as a precondition via future statutory reform, since no structural closure model is locked in
strengths: Avoids the specific 'pure consolidation shock' achievement disruption documented in Finland's 1999-2000 closures, since no closures are structurally mandated (F-live-finnish_reform-06)

Preserves current between-school segregation configuration rather than actively restructuring provision in ways that could replicate Finnish metropolitan segregation dynamics (F-live-finnish_reform-07)
threats: Absent codified criteria, Hungary risks the same abrupt discontinuity Finland saw in 1995-2003 when protective policy was withdrawn under fiscal pressure, but without ever having had the initial equality-oriented comprehensive commitment (F-live-finnish_reform-01, F-live-finnish_reform-03)

Contested segregation effects from decentralization/choice, seen in Finland, could compound with Hungary's own documented faith-based selection dynamics under continued drift (F-live-finnish_reform-07, F-live-finnish_reform-09)
weaknesses: Lacks the rule-based, comprehensive-provision precondition that made Finland's 1972-1977 reform durable and legitimate (F-live-finnish_reform-01)

Removes the kind of deliberate, reversible protective policy (e.g., Finland's 1980s low pupil-minimum) that could buffer rural schools; instead leaves survival to uneven informal mobilization (F-live-finnish_reform-02)

Does not address the segregation-amplifying growth of church-sector operators, a dynamic paralleling Finnish choice-driven sorting concerns even though causality is contested there (F-live-finnish_reform-09, F-live-finnish_reform-07)
